does: This paper introduces RTS-Attack, an automated framework that jailbreaks LLMs by constructing nested scenarios that are semantically relevant to target queries while incorporating targeted toxic knowledge. The method systematically builds contextual narratives where harmful requests emerge naturally from the scenario setup rather than appearing as explicit malicious queries. The authors verify through experiments that this approach successfully bypasses alignment defenses across multiple LLMs while generating prompts that lack overtly harmful language, making the attack less detectable than prior nested scenario methods.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature on jailbreak attacks against LLMs, particularly extending nested scenario strategies that have shown promise in evading safety mechanisms. It responds to limitations in existing nested scenario approaches, which remain conspicuous due to their transparent malicious intent. The paper connects to research on LLM alignment and robustness evaluation, building on defenses like RLHF and constitutional AI that this work demonstrates can be vulnerable to semantically coherent attack patterns. It also relates to broader adversarial ML research examining how neural models can be fooled through contextual manipulation rather than direct adversarial input.
enables: This research opens directions for both understanding and improving LLM safety mechanisms. It highlights a specific vulnerability class in alignment defenses that future safety work must address, motivating development of more sophisticated detection methods and training approaches robust to semantically relevant but harmful scenario construction. The findings could drive advances in adversarial training that accounts for contextual coherence rather than treating prompts in isolation. Beyond safety, the techniques may inform more nuanced evaluations of model alignment and guide the design of guardrails that maintain utility while resisting attacks embedding harm within plausible narratives.
